question: What would be the angular separation between the consecutive bright fringes in Young's double slit experiment with blue-green light of wavelength  $400{\text{ }}nm$ ? The separation between the slits is $0.001{\text{ }}m.$
explanation:
As we know that, $\lambda=d \sin \theta$ and for very small value of $\theta, \sin \theta \approx \theta$
$\therefore \quad \theta=\dfrac{\lambda}{d}=\dfrac{400 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{3}}=4 \times 10^{-4} {rad}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: When Young's double slit experiment is performed in air, the $Y$ - coordinates of central maxima and $10^{\text {th }}$ maxima are  $2{\text{ }}cm$  and  $5{\text{ }}cm,$  respectively. If the apparatus is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.5, the corresponding $Y$ - coordinates will be
explanation:
Given,
In air YDSE's central maxima
$Y$-coordinate $=2 {~cm}$
$10^{\text {th }}$ maxima $=5 {~cm}$
$\eta=$ Liquid's refractive index $=1.5=3 / 2$
$\eta=\dfrac{\lambda_{\text {vacuum }}}{\lambda_{\text {medium }}^{\prime}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\text {medium }}^{\prime}=\dfrac{2}{3} \lambda_{\text {vacuum }}$
For a given co-ordinate system position of central maxima will remain unchanged
( $Y^{\prime}=2 {~cm}$ ) and ( $n=0$ )
Now, $\dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta Y^{\prime}}=\dfrac{10 \lambda D / d}{10 \lambda^{\prime} D / d}=\dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda / \mu}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{5-2}{Y^{\prime}-2}=\dfrac{3}{2} \Rightarrow Y^{\prime}=4 {~cm}$
New coordinates will be $2 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}$.
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: Two slits are made one millimetre apart and the screen is placed one metre away from the slits. The fringe width when light of wavelength    $500{\text{ }}nm$  is used is
explanation:
Given, $d=1 {~mm}=10^{-3} {~m}$
$D=1 m$
$\lambda=500 {~nm}=5 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
Fringe width,
$\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d}=\dfrac{1 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-3}}=5 \times 10^{-4} {~m}$
$=0.5 \times 10^{-3} {~m}=0.5 {~mm}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: Two slits separated by a distance of  $1{\text{ }}mm$  are illuminated with light of wavelength $6.5 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$. The interference fringes are observed on a screen placed at  $1{\text{ }}m$  from the slits. The distance between the third dark fringe and the fifth bright fringe is equal to
explanation:
Given, $d=1 {~mm}=1 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$
$\lambda=6.5 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
$D=1 {~m}$
Position of third dark fringes,
$x_{3}=(2 n-1) \dfrac{D \lambda}{2 d}$
$=(2 \times 3-1) \dfrac{D \lambda}{2 d}=\dfrac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}$
Position of fifth bright fringe,
$x_{5}=n \dfrac{D \lambda}{d}=5 \dfrac{D \lambda}{d}$
$\therefore \quad x_{5}-x_{3}=\dfrac{5 D \lambda}{d}-\dfrac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}$
$=\dfrac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}=\dfrac{5 \times 1 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}=1.625 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$
$=1.625 {~mm}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: A Young's double slit experiment apparatus has slits separated by  $0.2{\text{ }}mm$  and a screen  $60{\text{ }}cm$  away from the slits. The whole apparatus is immersed in a liquid medium of refractive index 11/9 and the slits are illuminated with green light $\lambda_{2}=550 {~nm}$ in vacuum). Find the fringe width of the pattern formed on the screen.
explanation:
In Young's double slit experiment,
$d=0.2 {~mm}=2 \times 10^{-4} {~m}$
$D=60 {~cm}=0.6 {~m}$
$\lambda=550 {~nm}=5.5 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$ and $\eta=\dfrac{11}{9}$
$\therefore$ Fringe width when whole apparatus is immersed in liquid,
$\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d \eta}=\dfrac{0.6 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{11}{9}} m$
$ = \frac{{9 \times 0.6 \times 5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{2 \times 11}}m$ $ = 1.35 \times {10^{ - 3}}m = 1.35\,mm$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: In Young's double slit experiment for what order does the wavelength of red light ($\lambda=780 {~nm}$) coincide with ($n+1$) th order of blue light $(\lambda=520 {~nm})$ ?
explanation:
Given, wavelength of red light,
$\lambda_{R}=780 {~nm}=7.8 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
Wavelength of blue light,
$\lambda_{B}=520 {~nm}=5.2 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
According to question,
$x_{R}=x_{B} \Rightarrow \dfrac{n \lambda_{R} D}{d}=\dfrac{(n+1) \lambda_{B} D}{d}$
$\Rightarrow n \lambda_{R}=(n+1) \lambda_{B} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{R}}{\lambda_{B}}=\dfrac{n+1}{n}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{-7}}{5.2 \times 10^{-7}}=\dfrac{n+1}{n} \Rightarrow \dfrac{39}{26}=\dfrac{n+1}{n}$
$\Rightarrow 39 n=26 n+26 \Rightarrow 13 n=26 \Rightarrow n=2$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In a double slit experiment performed in air, the angular width of a fringe is found to be $0.15^{\circ}$ on a screen placed  $80{\text{ }}cm$  away. The wavelength of light is used  $490{\text{ }}nm.$  The angular width of the fringe, if the entire apparatus is immersed in a medium of refractive index $5 / 3$ is
explanation:
In Young's double slit experiment,
Angular fringe width, $\theta=0.15^{\circ}$
$D=80 {~cm}=0.8 {~m}$
Wavelength of light used,
$\lambda=490 {~nm}=4.9 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
Angular fringe width when entire apparatus is immersed in a medium of refractive index
$\eta=5 / 3$, is given as, $\theta^{\prime}=\dfrac{\theta}{\eta}$
$=\dfrac{0.15}{5 / 3}=\dfrac{0.15 \times 3}{5}=0.09^{\circ}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: In an interference pattern of Young's double slit experiment, at a point we observe the  ${12^{{\text{th}}}}$  order maximum for a monochromatic lightsource with wavelength  $6000$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $.  What order will be visible here, if the source is replaced by a light of wavelength  $4800$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $ ?
explanation:
In Young's double slit experiment,
${n_1} = 12,{\lambda _1} = 6000$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,m$
${\lambda _2} = 4800$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ $ = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,m$
Then, $n_{2}=$ ?
In Young's double slit experiment, position of $n^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe,
$x_{n}=\dfrac{n D \lambda}{d}$
$\Rightarrow x_{n}=\dfrac{n_{1} D \lambda_{1}}{d}=\dfrac{n_{2} D \lambda_{2}}{d}$
$\Rightarrow n_{1} \lambda_{1}=n_{2} \lambda_{2}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}=\dfrac{n_{1} \lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{12 \times 6 \times 10^{-7}}{4.8 \times 10^{-7}}=15$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, if the distance between two slits is reduced by a factor of 2 and the wavelength of light is increased 4 times then the distance between two maxima will become  ______  times the original value
explanation:
Distance between the two maxima is equal to fringe width which is given as
$\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d} \Rightarrow \quad \beta \propto \dfrac{\lambda}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \times \dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}=\dfrac{4 \lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1}} \times \dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{1} / 2}=4 \times 2=8$
$\Rightarrow \quad \beta_{2}=8 \beta_{1}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment using monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$, the intensity of light at a point on the screen, where the path difference is $\lambda / 3$, is $I_{0}$. What is the intensity of light at a point, where the path difference is $\lambda$ ?
explanation:
Given, the intensity of light $=I_{0}$, at path difference, $\Delta x_{1}=\lambda / 3$.
Let intensity be $I$, at path difference, $\Delta x_{2}=\lambda$
Resultant intensity $I_{R}=I_{m} \cdot \cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad I_{R} \propto \cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \dfrac{I_{0}}{I}=\dfrac{\cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}}{2}\right)}{\cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{\phi_{2}}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{\cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\right)}{\cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \lambda \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{0}}{I}=\dfrac{\cos ^{2} \pi / 3}{\cos ^{2} \pi}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{0}}{I}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow I=4 I_{0}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: White light is used to illuminate the two slits in a Young's double experiment. The observed central fringe is
explanation: White light is used to illuminate the two slits in a Young's double slit experiment. The central point of the fringe pattern is at the same distance from the two coherent sources. Hence, at this point, each of the colours (white light consists of seven different colours) maintain same phase differences. Therefore, the observed central fringe is white. So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: On using red light  $(\lambda  = 6600$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $)   in Young's double slit experiment, 60 fringes are observed in the field of view. If violet light  $(\lambda  = 4400$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $)   is used, the number of fringes observed will be
explanation:
Given, ${\lambda _7} = 6600$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $, ${\lambda _2} = 4400$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $, ${n_1} = 60$
For same set up of YDSE, if $n$ be the number of fringes observed, then $n \alpha \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
$\therefore n_{1} \lambda_{1}=n_{2} \lambda_{2}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}=\dfrac{n_{1} \lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{60 \times 6600}{4400}=\dfrac{60 \times 6}{4}$
$=15 \times 6=90$ fringes
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: If in a Young's double slit experiment the slit separation is doubled and the distance of the screen from the slits is reduced to half, then the fringe widths become how many times their original value?
explanation:
Fringe width in Young's double slit experiment is given as
$\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d}$
where, $D=$ distance between source and screen
and $d=$ slit width.
$\therefore \dfrac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \times \dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\dfrac{d_{1}}{2 d_{1}} \times \dfrac{D_{1} / 2}{D_{1}}$
$\left[\because \quad d_{2}=2 d_{1}\right. $ and $\left. D_{2}=\dfrac{D_{1}}{2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow B_{2}=\dfrac{\beta_{1}}{4}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, $m^{\text {th }}$ order and $n^{\text {th }}$ order of bright fringes are formed at point $P$ on a distant screen, if monochromatic source of wavelength  $400\,nm$  and  $600\,nm$   are used respectively. The minimum value of $m$ and $n$ are respectively,
explanation:
Let $m^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe of wavelength $\lambda_{m}$ and $n^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe of wave length $\lambda_{n}$ formed at a distance $x_{n}$ from central maxima at same point $P$, i. e. , they coincide, therefore, $x_{m}=x_{n}$. $\dfrac{m \lambda_{m} D}{d}=\dfrac{n \lambda_{n} D}{d} \Rightarrow \dfrac{m}{n}=\dfrac{\lambda_{n}}{\lambda_{m}}$
Putting the given values, we get $=\dfrac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{400 \times 10^{-9}}$ [Given, $\lambda_{m}=400 \times 10^{-9} m, \lambda_{n}=600 \times 10^{-9} m$ ] $\therefore \quad \dfrac{m}{n}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
So, least integral values of $m$ and $n$ satisfying above requirement are $m=3$ and $n=2$. So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, a thin sheet of refractive index 1.6 is used to cover one slit while a thin the sheet of refractive index 1.3 is used to cover the second slit. The thickness of both the sheets are same and the wavelength of light used is  $600\,nm.$   If the central point on   the screen is now occupied by what had been the $10^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe ($m=10$), then the thickness of covering sheets is
explanation:
Given, refractive index of first sheet
$\eta_{1}=1.6$ refractive index of second sheet
$\eta_{2}=1.3$ and wavelength of light,
$\lambda=600 {~nm}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}$
Experimental setup of Young's double slit experiment is shown below, in which two thin sheets cover the slits.
So, the path difference introduced in slit 1 .
$\Delta {x_1} = \left( {{\eta _1} - 1} \right)t = (1.6 - 1)t = 0.6t$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952860/preptracker/questions/q_112566_explanation_1764952860424.png Similarly, path difference introduced in slit 2,
$\Delta x_{2}=\left(\eta_{2}-1\right) t=(1.3-1) t=0.3 t$
So, the net path difference introduced in central maxima,
$\Delta x_{\text {central maxima }}=\Delta x_{1}-\Delta x_{2}=0.6 t-0.3 t=0.3 t$
For central maxima, which occupied the $10{ }^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe,
$\Delta x_{c e n}=10 \lambda \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{10 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{0.3}=20 \mu {~m}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Transparent Slab Placed Infront of a Screen in YDSE

question: Light consisting of a plane wave of wavelength, $\lambda_{1}=8 \times 10^{-5} {~cm}$ and $\lambda_{2}=6 \times 10^{-5} {~cm}$ generates an interference pattern in Young's double slit experiment. If $n_{1}$ denotes the $n_{1}^{\text {th }}$ dark fringe due to light of wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ which coincides with $n_{2}{ }^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe due to light of wavelength $\lambda_{2}$, then
explanation:
Given, $\lambda_{1}=8 \times 10^{-5} {~cm}$ and
$\lambda_{2}=6 \times 10^{-5} {~cm}$
Position $n_{1}^{\text {th }}$, dark fringe (due to light of wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ ) is given by,
$x_{n_{1}}=\left(2 n_{1}+1\right) \dfrac{D \lambda_{1}}{2 d}$
Position of $n_{2}{ }^{\text {th }}$ of bright fringe due to light of
wavelength $\lambda_{2}$ is given by
$x_{n_{2}}=\dfrac{n_{2} D \lambda_{2}}{d}$
Since both the fringes coincide,
$\left(2 n_{1}+1\right) \dfrac{D \lambda_{1}}{2 d}=\dfrac{n_{2} D \lambda_{2}}{d}$
$\dfrac{2 n_{1}+1}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{2 \lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\dfrac{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-5}}{8 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\dfrac{2 n_{1}+1}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
$4{n_1} + 2 = 3{n_2} \Rightarrow 4{n_1} - 3{n_2} = - 2\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
when $n_{1}=1$ and $n_{2}=2$, then Eq. (1) is satisfied. Hence, for $n_{1}=1$ and $n_{2}=2$, given fringes coincide. So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: Interference fringes are obtained in a Young's double slit experiment using beam of light consisting two wavelengths  $500{\text{ }}nm$  and  $600{\text{ }}nm.$  Bright fringes due to both wavelengths coincide at  $2.5{\text{ }}mm$  from the central maximum. If the separation between the slits is   $3{\text{ }}mm,$  then the distance between the screen and plane of the slits is
explanation:
Position of bright fringe on screen is given by
$x = \frac{{n\lambda D}}{d}(n = 1,2,3,...)$
For coinciding case, $n_{1} \lambda_{1}=n_{2} \lambda_{2}$
$\therefore n=6$ for wavelength of 500 nm
and $n=5$ for wavelength of 600 nm .
Using any one of those,
$2.5 \times 10^{-3}=\dfrac{6 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times D}{3 \times 10^{-3}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad D=2.5 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, a monochromatic light of wavelength 600$nm$  is used. If the two slits are covered by transparent sheets of thickness  $0.132{\text{ }}mm$  and  $0.1{\text{ }}mm$  of refractive index 1.5, then the number of fringes that will Shift-due to introduction of the sheets are
explanation:
Number of fringes Shifted-due to extra path difference caused by transparent slab is
$N=\dfrac{(n-1) t}{\lambda}$
where $m$ is the reflective indicates of material.
So, when two plates of thickness $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ are placed in front of slits, then Shift-is
$N=N_{2}-N_{1}=\dfrac{(n-1)\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}{\lambda}$
$=\dfrac{(1.5-1)(0.132-0.1) \times 10^{-3}}{600 \times 10^{-9}}$
$=\dfrac{0.5 \times 0.032 \times 10^{6}}{600}=27$ fringes
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Transparent Slab Placed Infront of a Screen in YDSE

question: In an experiment, light passing through two slits separated by a distance of  $0.3{\text{ }}mm$  is projected on to a screen placed at  $1m$  from the plane of slits. It is observed that the distance between the central fringe and the adjacent bright fringe is   $1.9\,mm.$  The wavelength of light in  $nm$  is
explanation:
Distance between central bright fringe and next bright fringe is width of one dark fringe $\beta$
So, $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$
$\Rightarrow 1.9 \times 10^{-3}=\dfrac{\lambda \times 1}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=57 \times 10^{-8} {~m}$
$=570 \times 10^{-9} {~m}=570 {~nm}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: If whole YDSE apparatus is immersed in a liquid of refractive index $\mu$, then what is the effect on fringe width?
explanation:
The fringe width in a YDSE is
$\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$
In liquid medium $\beta^{1}=\dfrac{\lambda_{m} D}{d}=\dfrac{\lambda D}{\mu d}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{1} < \beta$
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: It may increase on one side and decrease on other side

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Fringe width of red light is more than fringe width of violet light.
Fringe width is directly proportional to the wavelength of light used. Choose the correct option.
explanation:
We know that
Fringe width $(\beta)=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$
So, statement B is correct.
We know that $\lambda_{\text {Red }}>\lambda_{\text {violet }} \Rightarrow \beta_{\text {Red }}>\beta_{\text {violet }}$
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: Statement - A is correct and statement - B is incorrect
Both statement-A and statement - B are correct
Statement-A is incorrect and statement - B is correct
Both statement -A and statement -B are incorrect

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, light from two identical sources are superimposing on a screen. The path difference between the two lights reaching at a point on the screen is $7 \dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.  The ratio of intensity of fringe at this point with respect to the maximum intensity of the fringe is
explanation:
The path difference between the two lights reaching at a point on the screen is $\Delta x=\dfrac{7 \lambda}{4}$;
The phase difference between the two lights is given by
$\Delta \phi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \cdot \Delta \,x = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \cdot \frac{{7\lambda }}{4} = \frac{{7\pi }}{2}$
$I = {I_{\max }}{\cos ^2}(\phi /2)$
$ \Rightarrow I = {I_{\max }}{\cos ^2}\left( {\frac{{7\pi }}{{2 \times 2}}} \right)$
$\frac{I}{{{I_{\max }}}} = {\cos ^2}\left( {2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}} \right) = {\cos ^2}\frac{\pi }{4} = \frac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: The width of one of the two slits in a Young's double slit experiment is 4 times that of the other slit. The ratio of the maximum to the minimum intensities in the interference pattern is
explanation:
Intensity $\propto$ width of slit
If $I_{1}=I$
$\therefore I_{2}=4 I$
$I_{\text {max }}=\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}=(\sqrt{I}+2 \sqrt{I})^{2}=9 I$
$I_{\min }=\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}=(\sqrt{I}-2 \sqrt{I})^{2}=I$
so, $\dfrac{I_{\text {max }}}{I_{\text {min }}}=9$
Therefore, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: A monochromatic beam of light of 6000 $ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ is used in $YDSE$ set-up. The two slits are covered with two thin films of equal thickness ${t}$ but of different refractive indices as shown in the figure. Considering the intensity of the incident beam on the slits to be ${I_{0}}$, find the point on the screen at which intensity is ${I_{0}}$ and is just above the central maxima. (Assume that there is no change in intensity of the light after passing through the films.) Consider ${t=6 \mu {m}, d=1 {~mm}}$, and ${D=1 {~m}}$, where ${d}$ and ${D}$ have their usual meaning. Give your answer in $mm$.
explanation:
$I=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\phi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \dfrac{\phi}{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$
Therefore, path difference of the two beams producing intensity $I_{0}$ on the screen is $\lambda / 3$.
Optical path difference between two beams meeting on the screen is
$\Delta x = \left\{ {\left( {{S_2}P - t + \frac{4}{3}t} \right) - \left( {{S_1}P - t + \frac{3}{2}t} \right)} \right\}$
$ = \left( {{S_2}P - {S_1}P} \right) + t\left( {\frac{4}{3} - \frac{3}{2}} \right)$
$\Delta x = d\,\,\sin \,\,\theta - \frac{1}{6}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{d y}{D}-\dfrac{t}{6}=\dfrac{\lambda}{3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d y}{D}=\dfrac{\lambda}{3}+\dfrac{t}{6}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{D}{d}\left[ {\frac{\lambda }{3} + \frac{t}{6}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}\left( {\frac{{6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{3} + \frac{{0.6 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{6}} \right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} \times 3 \times 10^{-7}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m}$
$\therefore \quad y=30 {~mm}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Transparent Slab Placed Infront of a Screen in YDSE

question:
In a modified Young's double slit experiment, a monochromatic uniform and parallel beam of light of wavelength 6000 $ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ and intensity ${\dfrac{10}{\pi} {W} / {m}^{2}}$ is incident normally on two circular aperture ${A}$ and ${B}$ of radii 0.001 $m$ and 0.002 $m$ respectively. A perfect transparent film of thickness 2000$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ and refractive index 1.5 for the wavelength of 6000$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ is placed in front of aperture ${A}$ as shown in figure.

Calculate the intensity of light (in ${\mu {W}}$ ) received at the focal point of the lens in watt. The lens is symmetrically placed with respect to the aperture. Assume that ${10 \%}$ of the power received by each aperture goes in the original direction and is brought to the focal point.
explanation:
The intensities of light from the sources $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are given as
$\begin{aligned}& I_{1}=\left(\dfrac{10}{\pi}\right) \times \pi(0.01)^{2}=10^{-5} {~W} \\& I_{2}=\left(\dfrac{10}{\pi}\right) \times \pi(0.02)^{2}=4 \times 10^{-5} {~W}\end{aligned}$
The intensities of sources after emerging from the lenses are
$\begin{aligned}& I_{A}=0.10 \times 10^{-5} {~W}=10^{-6} {~W} \\& I_{B}=0.10 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} {~W}=4 \times 10^{-6} {~W}\end{aligned}$
The path difference produced due to film
$\Delta x = (\mu - 1)t = (1.5 - 1) \times 2000 \times {10^{ - 10}}$
$ = {10^{ - 7}}\,m \Rightarrow \phi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \times \Delta x$
$ = \frac{{2\pi }}{{6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}} \times {10^{ - 7}}$
$ \Rightarrow \phi = \frac{\pi }{2}{\text{ radian}}$
As refraction through lens does not introduce any path difference so above will be the net phase difference in the two light beams superposing at focal point of the lens. So net intensity received at $F$ is given as
$I = {I_A} + {I_B} + 2\sqrt {{I_A}{I_B}} \cos \phi $
$ = {10^{ - 6}} + 4 \times {10^{ - 6}} + \sqrt {{{10}^{ - 6}} \times 4 \times {{10}^6}} \cos \frac{\pi }{3}$
$ = 7 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on YDSE

question: In a $YDSE$ bi-chromatic light of wavelengths 400 $nm$ and 560 $nm$ are used. The distance between the slits is 0.1 $mm$ and the distance between the plane of the slits and the screen is 1 $m$ . Find the minimum distance between two successive regions of complete darkness
explanation:
At the area of total darkness, in double slit apparatus, minima will occur for both the wavelengths which are incident simultaneously and normally.
$\left( {\frac{{2n + 1}}{2}} \right){\lambda _1} = \frac{{(2m + 1)}}{2}{\lambda _2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2n + 1}}{{2m + 1}} = \frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2n + 1}}{{2m + 1}}$$ = \frac{{560}}{{400}} = \frac{7}{5}{\text{ or }}10n = 14m + 2$
By inspection, the two solutions are
(i) If $m_{1}=2, n_{1}=3$ (ii) If $m_{2}=7, n_{2}=10$.
$\therefore$ Distance $\Delta S=\dfrac{D \lambda_{1}}{d}\left[\dfrac{\left(2 n_{2}+1\right)-\left(2 n_{1}+1\right)}{2}\right]$
Now putting $n_{2}=10$ and $n_{1}=3$
$\Delta S=4 \times 7 \times 10^{-3} {~m} \Rightarrow \Delta S=28 {~mm}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question:
On introducing a thin mica sheet of thickness ${2 \times 10^{-6} {~m}}$ and refractive index 1.5 in the path of one of the waves, central bright maxima shifts by ${n}$ fringes. Wavelength of the wave used is
$5000$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $, then the value of ${n}$ is
explanation:
Shift in number of fringes is given by ${n \lambda=(\mu-1) t}$
$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{{(\mu - 1)t}}{\lambda }$
$ = \frac{{(1.5 - 1) \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{5000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}} = 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Transparent Slab Placed Infront of a Screen in YDSE

question: In a typical Young's double slit experiment a point source of monochromatic light is kept as shown in the figure. If the source is given an instantaneous velocity ${v=1 {~mm}}$ per second towards the screen, then the instantaneous velocity of central maxima is given as ${\alpha \times 10^{-\beta} {cm} / {s}}$ upward in scientific notation. The value of ${\alpha+\beta}$ is
explanation:
The central maxima
$\dfrac{d y}{D}=\sqrt{d^{2}+x^{2}}-x=x\left[1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 x^{2}}\right]-x=\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 x}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955607/preptracker/questions/q_32932_explanation_1764955607232.png $y=\dfrac{D d}{2 x} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d y}{d t}=-\dfrac{D d}{2 x^{2}}\left(\dfrac{d x}{d t}\right)$
$=\left(\dfrac{1 \times 0.01}{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}\right) \times(0.001)=0.02 {~mm} / {s}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad y=2 \times 10^{-3} {~cm} / {s} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha+\beta=5\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on YDSE

question: Two circular aperture ${S_{1}}$, and ${S_{2}}$ of radii $1\,m$ and $2\,mm$. A parallel beam of light ${\dfrac{7}{22} {Wm}^{-2}}$ and wavelength $600\,nm$ is incident normally as shown in the figure. A transparent film of thickness $200\,nm$ having refractive index ${\mu_{1}=2}$ is placed in front of ${S_{1}}$, and other transparent film of thickness $200\,nm$ and refractive index 1.5 is placed in front of other slit. The resultant power  at the centre of screen is
explanation:
${\Delta x=\left(\mu_{1}-1\right) t_{1}-\left(\mu_{2}-1\right) t_{2}}$
$ = (2 - 1)200 - (1.5 - 1)200$
$ = 200 - 100 = 100\,nm$
${\therefore \quad \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x=\dfrac{2 \pi}{600} \times 100=\dfrac{\pi}{3}}$
${\therefore \quad P=P_{1}+P_{2}+2 \sqrt{P_{1} P_{2}} \cos \theta}$
$ = {I_0}\pi r_1^2 + {I_0}\pi r_2^2 + 2\sqrt {{I_0}\pi r_1^2 \times {I_0}\pi r_2^2} \cos \frac{\pi }{3}$
$ = 7\mu W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on YDSE

question:
In a Young's double slit experiment, the two slits are illuminated by a monochromatic light source ${S}$ of wavelength $400\,nm$.

If $S{S_1} = 10\,\mu m$ and $S{S_2} = 50\,\mu m,$ the number of maxima formed on screen is
explanation:
Here, $\Delta x = d\sin \theta - \left( {S{S_2} - S{S_1}} \right)$
$ = d\sin \theta - 40\mu m = m\lambda $
or ${\quad d \sin \theta=m \lambda+40 \mu {m}}$
or ${\sin \theta=\dfrac{m \lambda}{d}+40 \times 10^{-6} {~m}}$
${\because \quad-1 < \sin \theta < 1}$
or ${\quad-1 < \dfrac{m \lambda}{d}+40 \times 10^{-6} < 1}$
or ${-1 < \dfrac{m \times 400 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}+40 \times 10^{-6} < 1}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { or } & -1 < 4 {~m} \times 10^{-2}+40 \times 10^{-6} < 1 \\\text { or } & -1 < (1000 {~m}+1) 40 \times 10^{-6} < 1 \\\text { or } & -2500 < 1000 {~m}+1 < 2500 \\\text { or } & -2.5 < m < 2.5 \\\text { or } \therefore & m=-2,-1,0,1,2\end{array}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Maximum Fringes Formed on a screen in YDSE

question: A screen is at a distance ${D}=80 {~cm}$ from a diaphragm having two narrow slits ${S}_{1}$ and ${S}_{2}$ which are ${d}=2 {~mm}$ apart. Slit ${S}_{1}$ is covered by a transparent sheet of thickness $t_{1}=2.5 \,\mu {m}$ and slit ${S}_{2}$ is covered by another sheet of thickness ${t}_{2}=1.25 \mu {m}$. (as in figure). Both sheets are made of same material having refractive index $\mu_{{s}}=1.40$. Water is filled in the space between diaphragm and screen. A monochromatic light beam of wavelength $\lambda  = 5000$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $ is incident normally on the diaphragm. Assuming intensity of beam to be uniform, calculate the ratio of intensity of ${C}$ to maximum intensity of interference pattern obtained on the screen $\left(\mu_{{w}}=\dfrac{4}{3}\right)$.
explanation:
Path difference at $C$,
$\Delta x = {t_1}(\mu - 1) - {t_2}(\mu - 1)$
$ = \left( {{t_1} - {t_2}} \right)(\mu - 1)$
$ = \left( {{t_1} - {t_2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{\mu _s}}}{{{\mu _w}}} - 1} \right)$
$ = (2.5 - 1.25)\left( {\frac{{14 \times 3}}{{4 \times 10}} - 1} \right)\mu m$
$ = \frac{{25}}{{400}}\mu m$
$ = \frac{1}{{16}}\mu m$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta {x}$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi \times 4}{5000 \times 10^{-10} \times 3} \times \dfrac{1}{16} \times 10^{-6}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \lambda ' = \frac{\lambda }{\mu }} \right)$
$=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$
$\Rightarrow {I}_{\max }=4 {I}_{0}$
Intensity at ${C}, {I}_{{c}}=2 {I}_{0}\left(1+\cos \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)=3 {I}_{0}$
$\therefore$ Required ratio $=\dfrac{{I}_{{c}}}{{I}_{\max }}=\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the interference pattern is found to have an intensity ratio between bright and dark fringes as 16. Then the ratio of their amplitudes will be
explanation:
$\frac{{{I_{\max }}}}{{{I_{{\text{min }}}}}} = {\left( {\frac{{\sqrt {{I_1}} + \sqrt {{I_2}} }}{{\sqrt {{I_1}} - \sqrt {{I_2}} }}} \right)^2}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{16}{1}=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}}{\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}}\right)^{2}$
$\therefore 4\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)=\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}$
$\therefore 5 \sqrt{I_{2}}=3 \sqrt{I_{1}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{{I}_{1}}{{I}_{2}}=\dfrac{25}{9}$
$\therefore \frac{{{A_1}^2}}{{\,{A_2}^2}} = \frac{{25}}{9}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$\left( {\because \,\,1 \propto {A^2}} \right)$
$\therefore \frac{{{A_1}}}{{\,{A_2}}} = \frac{5}{3} = 1.67$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a biprism experiment, the distance between the slits is $0.25 {~cm}$ and the distance of screen from slits is $120 {~cm}$. If the wavelength of light used is $6000$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $ and ${I}_{0}$ is the intensity of central maximum, then at what distance   from the central maxima, the intensity will be $\dfrac{{I}_{0}}{2}$ ?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949970/preptracker/questions/q_32862_explanation_1764949970553.png ${I}={I}_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\phi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{0}}{2}={I}_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\phi}{2}$
$\because \cos \dfrac{\phi}{2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,$$\dfrac{\phi}{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$
$\therefore \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
Since $\frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }\left( {\frac{{yd}}{D}} \right) = \frac{\pi }{2}$$\,\,\,\,\left( {{\rm{Path}}\,{\rm{difference = }}\frac{{yd}}{D}} \right)$
$\therefore y=\dfrac{\lambda D}{4 d}$
$ = \frac{{6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times 120 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{4 \times 0.25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$ = 7.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,m$
$ = 72\,\mu m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lloyd's Mirror and Fresnel's Biprism Experiment

question: Fringes are produced by a Fresnel biprism in the focal plane of an eye-piece which is at a distance of $1 {~m}$ from the centre slit. A lens inserted between the biprism and eye-piece gives two images of the slit in two positions. In first case, the images of the slit are $9 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$ and in the second position the images are $4 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$ apart. If the sodium light $\left(\lambda=6000 \times 10^{-10} {~m}\right)$ is used, then the distance between the interference fringes is found to be $1 \times 10^{-{M}} {m}$. What is the value of $M$? (Take $\pi=3.14$ )
explanation:
When the distance between the screen and the object is greater than four times the focal length of a convex lens, there are two positions of the lens where it forms a real image of the object on the screen. If ${d_1}$ and $d_{2}$ are the sizes of the images in the two positions then $d$ (size of the object) $=\sqrt{{d}_{1} {~d}_{2}}$
$\therefore {d}$ (distance between virtual sources)
$=\sqrt{9 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}$
But, $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda {D}}{{d}}$
$\therefore \beta = \frac{{6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times 1}}{{\sqrt {4 \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }}$
$ = \frac{{6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 1 \times {10^{ - 4}}$
$\therefore {M}=4$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lloyd's Mirror and Fresnel's Biprism Experiment

question:
A Fresnel biprism of angle $2^{\circ}$ is illuminated by light of wavelength $6280$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $ from a source which is $0.10 {~m}$ away from it. What will be the width of the fringes formed on a screen kept $0.9 {~m}$ away from the biprism?
(Take Refractive index of glass $ = 1.5,\pi = 3.14$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949969/preptracker/questions/q_32838_explanation_1764949969113.png The deviation $\delta$ produced by each half of biprism $=(\mu-1) A$
where, ${A}$ is in radian
Here,
$d=2(\mu-1) A x$
$=2(1.5-1)\left(\dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 2^{\circ}\right) \times 0.1$
${d}=\dfrac{\pi}{900} {~m}$
${D}=0.9+0.1=1 {~m}$
Now, $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$
$\beta = \frac{{\left( {6280 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right) \times 1}}{{\frac{\pi }{{900}}}}$
$ = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,m = 0.18\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lloyd's Mirror and Fresnel's Biprism Experiment

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the slits are horizontal. The intensity at a point ${P}$ (as shown in figure) is $\dfrac{3}{4} I_{m}$, where $I_{m}$ is the maximum intensity. If the value of $\theta$ is found to be $\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{N}{M}\right)$, then the value of $N+M$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955591/preptracker/questions/q_32824_explanation_1764955590687.png Here, $\dfrac{{I}}{{I}_{{m}}}=\dfrac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)=\dfrac{3}{4}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\because {I_R} = 4{I_0}{{\cos }^2}\left( {\frac{\phi }{2}} \right) = {I_m}{{\cos }^2}\left( {\frac{\phi }{2}} \right)} \right]$
$\Rightarrow \cos \dfrac{\phi}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\phi}{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$
From figure,
path difference, $d\cos \theta = 2\lambda \cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(\because d = 2\lambda )$
Phase difference $=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times$ path difference
$\therefore \dfrac{\pi}{3}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times 2 \lambda \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{12}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{12}\right)$
Comparing with $\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{N}{M}\right)$.
$\therefore {N}=1, {M}=12$
$\therefore {N}+{M}=13$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Perpendicular to the Screen

question: A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths, $650 {~nm}$ and $520 {~nm}$ is used to obtain interference fringes in a Young's double-slit experiment. What is the least distance   from a central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide? The distance between the slits is $3 {~mm}$ and the distance between the plane of the slits and screen is $150 {~cm}$.
explanation:
Let $'y'$ be the linear distance between the centre of screen and the point at which the bright fringes due to both wavelength coincides. Let ${n}_{1}^{\text {th }}$ number of bright fringe with wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ coincides with ${n}_{2}^{\text {th }}$ number of bright fringe with wavelength $\lambda_{2}$.
$\therefore n_{1} \beta_{1}=n_{2} \beta_{2}$
$\Rightarrow {n}_{1} \dfrac{\lambda_{1} {D}}{{d}}={n}_{2} \dfrac{\lambda_{2} {D}}{{d}}$
$\Rightarrow {n}_{1} \lambda_{1}={n}_{2} \lambda_{2}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also at first position, the ${n}^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe of one will coincide with $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ bright of other.
If $\lambda_{2} < \lambda_{1}$,
So then ${n}_{2}>{n}_{1}$
$\Rightarrow {n}_{2}={n}_{1}+1$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Using equation (2) and equation (1),
$n_{1} \lambda_{1}=\left(n_{1}+1\right) \lambda_{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left({n}_{1}\right)\left(650 \times 10^{-9}\right)=\left({n}_{1}+1\right)\left(520 \times 10^{-9}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 65 {n}_{1}=52 {n}_{1}+52$
$\Rightarrow 13 {n}_{1}=52$
$\Rightarrow {n}_{1}=4$
Thus, $y=n_{1} \beta_{1}$
$ = {n_1}\frac{{{\lambda _1}D}}{d}$
$ = 4\left[ {\frac{{\left( {6.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right)(1.5)}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right]$
$ = 1.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
$ = 1.3\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the wavelength of the light used is doubled and distance between two slits is made half of initial distance. The resultant fringe width becomes
explanation:
As we know that fringe width ${\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}}$
If ${\lambda_{f}=2 \lambda, d_{f}=\dfrac{d}{2}}$
${\therefore}$ Resultant fringe width ${\beta_{f}=\dfrac{\lambda_{f} D}{d_{f}}=\dfrac{2 \lambda \times D}{\dfrac{d}{2}}}$
$\beta_{f}=4 \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}=4 \beta$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In the Young's double slit experiment using sodium light ($\lambda  = 5898$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $), 92 fringes are seen. If the green colour light ($\lambda  = 5461$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $)  is used, how many fringes will be seen
explanation:
${\lambda _1} = 5898$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{n_1} = 92$
${\lambda _2} = 5461$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $,$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{n_2} = ?$
${\therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{5898}{5461}=\dfrac{n_{2}}{92}}$
${\Rightarrow n_{2}=\dfrac{5898}{5461} \times 92=99.362 \approx 99}$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: The focal length of a convex lens will be maximum for
explanation:
Since, focal length $ \propto \frac{1}{\mu }\quad \quad \quad (1)$
and $\mu \propto \frac{1}{\lambda }\quad \quad \quad (2)$
From (1) and (2)
Focal length ${\propto \lambda}$
${\therefore}$ Focal length is maximum for maximum ${\lambda}$
i.e., red light, option (4) is correct.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on YDSE

question: In a double slit experiment, ${5^{th{\rm{ }}}}$ dark fringe is formed opposite to one of the slits, the wavelength of light is
explanation:
${n^{th{\rm{ }}}}$ dark fringe is formed at
$y_{n}=(2 n-1) \dfrac{D \lambda}{2 d}$
$\therefore y_{5}=\dfrac{9 D \lambda}{2 d} \quad(\because n=5)$
Also, $y_{5}=\dfrac{d}{2}$
$\text { So, } \dfrac{d}{2}=\dfrac{9 D \lambda}{2 d} ; \lambda=\dfrac{d^{2}}{9 D}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the spacing between the slits is $d$ and wavelength of light used is $6000 \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $. If the angular width of a fringe formed on a distant screen is $1^{\circ}$, then value of $d$ is
explanation:
Angular fringe width, $\theta=\dfrac{\lambda}{d}$
or, $d=\dfrac{\lambda}{\theta}$
Here,$\lambda = 6000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } = 6000 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m$
$\begin{aligned}& \theta=1^{\circ}=\dfrac{\pi}{180} \mathrm{rad}
\\& \therefore \quad d=\dfrac{6000 \times 10^{-10}}{\pi / 180}\end{aligned}$
$ = 3.44 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,m = 0.03\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path different is $\lambda=I$. The intensity of light at point where the path difference becomes $\dfrac{\lambda}{3}$ is
explanation:
Resultant intensity, $I_{R}=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\phi}{2}$
Case I : $\Delta x=\lambda, \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \lambda=2 \pi$
$I_{R}=I$
So, $I_{R}=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2}(\pi)=4 I_{0} \Rightarrow I_{0}=\dfrac{I}{4}$
Case II : $\Delta x=\dfrac{\lambda}{3}, \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{3}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$.
So, $I_{R}^{\prime}=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}\right)=I_{0} \Rightarrow I_{R}^{\prime}=\dfrac{I}{4}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a Young's experiment, the separation between the slits is $0.10\,nm$, the wavelength of light used is $600\,nm$ and the interference pattern is observed on a screen $1.0\,m$ away. Find the separation between the successive bright fringes.
explanation:
The separation between the successive bright fringes is $\beta = \frac{{D\lambda }}{d} = \frac{{1 \times 600 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{{.1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \beta = 6.0\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: When a mica sheet of thickness 7 microns and $\mu=1.6$ is placed in the path of one of interfering beams in the biprism experiment then the central fringe gets at the position of seventh bright fringe. The wavelength of light used will be
subject: Physics
subtopic: Transparent Slab Placed Infront of a Screen in YDSE

question: In Young's double slit experiment 10th order maximum is obtained at the point of observation in the interference pattern for $\lambda  = 7000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } $. If the source is replaced by another one of wavelength $5000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } $ then the order of maximum at the same point will be-
explanation:
${n_1}{\lambda _1} = {n_2}{\lambda _2}$
$10 \times 7000 = {n_2} \times 5000 \Rightarrow {n_2} = 14$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: in a Young's double slit experiment, one of the slit is covered with a transparent sheet of thickness $3.6 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,cm$ due to which position of central fringe shifts to a position originally occupied by 30th bright fringe. The refractive index of the sheet, if $\lambda  = 6000\mathop A\limits^ \circ  $ is
subject: Physics

question: If white light is used in the Newton's rings experiment, the colour observed in the reflected light is complementary to that observed in the transmitted light through the same point. This is due to
explanation: Newton's ring is a phenomena in which an interference pattern is created by reflection of light between spherical surface and an adjacent touching flat surface. There is a phase reversal of $\pi$ at two places as reflected and transmitted part and total phase difference is 0 .The ray reflected at the upper surface of the air film suffers no phase change, while the ray reflected internally at the lower surface suffers a phase change of $\pi$.

options.text: $90^{\circ}$ change of phase in one of the reflected waves
$180^{\circ}$ change of phase in one of the reflected waves
$145^{\circ}$ change of phase in one of the reflected waves
$45^{\circ}$ change of phase in one of the reflected waves

subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on YDSE

question: If Young's double slit experiment is performed in water instead of air, then
explanation:
Fringe width, $\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d}$ or $\beta \propto \lambda$
If the Young's double slit experiment is performed in water, wavelength $\lambda$ decreases, so fringe width also decreases.
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: If the 8th bright band due to light of wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ coincides with 9th bright band from light of wavelength $\lambda_{2}$ in Young's double slit experiment, then the possible wavelengths of visible light are
explanation:
Position of $n$th bright fringe from
central maxima is $\frac{{n\lambda D}}{d}.$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{8{\lambda _1}D}}{d} = \frac{{9{\lambda _2}D}}{d} \Rightarrow \frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = \frac{9}{8}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{\text{given}}} \right)$
Hence, the possible wavelengths of
visible light is of the ratio of $9: 8$.
So option (4) is correct
because ratio of $\frac{{450\,nm}}{{400\,nm}} = \frac{9}{8}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the intensity of light coming from one of the slits is doubled the intensity from the other slit. The ratio of the maximum intensity to the minimum intensity in the interference fringe pattern observed is
explanation:
$\frac{{{I_{\max }}}}{{{I_{\min }}}} = \frac{{{{(\sqrt {2I} + \sqrt I )}^2}}}{{{{(\sqrt {2I} - \sqrt I )}^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 2 + 1}}{{\sqrt 2 - 1}}} \right)^2}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(given)}}$
Now, rationalising the denominator by
multiplying the denominator with
numerator and with denominator itself.
$ = {\left[ {\frac{{(\sqrt 2 + 1)(\sqrt 2 + 1)}}{{(\sqrt 2 - 1)(\sqrt 2 + 1)}}} \right]^2}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{{2 + 1 + 2\sqrt 2 }}{{2 - 1}}} \right)^2} = \frac{{9 + 8 + 12\sqrt 2 }}{1}$
$ = \,\,34$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, the separation between the two slits is $0.9\,mm$ and the fringe are observed $1\,m$ away. If it produces the second dark fringe at a distance of $1\,mm$ from the central fringe, the wavelength of the monochromatic source of light used is
explanation:
The distance between two consecutive dark fringes or bright fringes are recognised as $\beta$ (fringe width) and that between central fringe and first dark fringe on either side is $\beta / 2$.
Given, $d = 0.9\,mm = 0.9 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$ and $D = 1\,m$ Spacing between second dark fringe and central fringe
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \beta + \frac{\beta }{2} = \frac{{3\beta }}{2} = 1\,mm$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\beta = \frac{2}{3} \times 1\,mm$
$ \Rightarrow \,{\kern 1pt} \frac{{\lambda D}}{d} = \frac{2}{3}\,mm$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\lambda = \frac{2}{3} \times {10^{ - 3}} \times \frac{{0.9 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{1}$
$ = 600 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 600\,nm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width is $\beta$. If the entire arrangement is placed in a liquid of refractive index $n$, the fringe width becomes
explanation:
Fringe width,
$\beta = D\lambda /d\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1) (for air) }}$
When entire arrangement is placed in a
liquid of refractive index $n$, then it
becomes
${\beta ^\prime } = \frac{{D\lambda }}{{nd}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\frac{{{\beta ^\prime }}}{\beta } = \frac{{D\lambda }}{{nd}} \times \frac{d}{{D\lambda }}\, \Rightarrow {\beta ^\prime } = \frac{\beta }{n}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE Performed in Transparent Liquid Medium

question: White light is used to illuminate two slits in Young's double slit experiment. The separation between the slits is $b$ and the screen is at a distance $d(>>b)$ from the slits. At a point on the screen directly in front of one of the slits, which wavelengths are missing?
explanation:
The situation is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949965/preptracker/questions/q_32649_explanation_1764949964805.png The path difference between the waves reaching the point $P$ is
$\Delta p{\text{ }} = BP - AP$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = {\left( {{d^2} + {b^2}} \right)^{1/2}} - d$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = d{\left( {1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{d^2}}}} \right)^{1/2}} - d$
$\,\,\,\,\,\, \approx d\left[ {1 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{d^2}}}} \right] - d = \frac{{{b^2}}}{{2d}}$
For a dark band,
$\,\,\,\,\,\Delta p = \frac{{{b^2}}}{{2d}} = (2n - 1)\frac{\lambda }{2}$
where, $n = 1,2,3or\lambda = \frac{1}{{(2n - 1)}} \cdot \frac{{{b^2}}}{d}$
Hence, the missing wavelengths are
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{b^2}}}{d},\frac{{{b^2}}}{{3d}},\frac{{{b^2}}}{{5d}},..$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In a YDS setup, the distance between the plane of the slits and the screen is increased by ${20 \%}$ and the distance between the slits is increased by ${50 \%}$. Then the percentage change in fringe width is
explanation:
${\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d}}$
${D^\prime } = D + \frac{{20}}{{100}}D = 1.2D;$
${d^\prime } = d + \frac{{50}}{{100}}d = 1.5d$
$\therefore {\beta ^\prime } = \frac{{{D^\prime }\lambda }}{{{d^\prime }}} = \frac{{D(1.2) \cdot \lambda }}{{d(1.5)}}D/d$
$ = 0.8\lambda = 0.8\beta $
${\therefore}$ Percentage charge ${\left(1-\dfrac{\beta^{\prime}}{\beta}\right) 100}$
${=(1-0.8) \times 100=20 \%}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: Two slits are separated by a distance of $0.5\,mm$ and illuminated with light of $\lambda  = 6000$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $. If the screen is placed $2.5\,m$ from the slits, the distance of the third bright image from the center will be
explanation:
Distance of the ${n^{\text {th }}}$ bright fringe from the centre ${X_{n}=\dfrac{n \lambda D}{d}}$
$ \Rightarrow {X_3} = \frac{{3 \times 6000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times 2.5}}{{0.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
$ = 9 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m = 9\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: Two coherent light sources ${P}$ and ${Q}$ each of wavelength ${\lambda}$ are separated by a distance ${3 \lambda}$ as shown. The maximum number of minima formed on line ${A B}$ which runs from ${-\infty}$ to ${+\infty}$ is
explanation:
For minima, ${d \sin \theta=\left(n-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \lambda}$
${\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\left(n-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \dfrac{\lambda}{d}}$
as ${\sin \theta \leq 1}$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {n - \frac{1}{2}} \right)\frac{\lambda }{d} \leqslant 1\,\,\,i.e.,\,\,\left( {n - \frac{1}{2}} \right) \leqslant \frac{d}{\lambda }$
$i.e$ ${n \leq \dfrac{d}{\lambda}+\dfrac{1}{2}}$ $i.e$ ${n \leq \dfrac{3 \lambda}{\lambda}+\dfrac{1}{2}}$ $i.e$ ${n \leq \dfrac{7}{2}}$
$i.e$ ${n=1,2,3}$
So, we will have 3 dark fringe on one side above central maxima.
If we consider both side it will be ${(3+3)}$, $i.e$, 6
subject: Physics
subtopic: Maximum Fringes Formed on a screen in YDSE

question: The distance between two coherent sources is $0.1\,mm$. The fringe-width on a screen $1.2\,m$ away from the source is $6.0\,mm$. The wavelength of light used is
explanation:
We have ${d=0.1 {~mm}=10^{-4} {~m}, D=1.2 {~m}}$, ${\beta=6 {~mm}=6 \times 10^{-3} {~m}}$
${\beta=\dfrac{D \lambda}{d} \Rightarrow 6 \times 10^{-3}=\dfrac{1.2 \times \lambda}{10^{-4}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 5 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,m = 5000$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the wavelength of red light is $7800$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $ and that of blue light is $5200$ $ \mathop A^{~~\circ}  $. The value of ${n}$ for which ${n^{\text {th }}}$ bright band due to red light coincides with ${(n+1)^{\text {th }}}$ bright band due to blue light, is
explanation:
We have,
${y_{n}(}$ Red ${)=y_{n+1}(}$ Blue ${)}$
${\Rightarrow 7800 n=5200(n+1)\left[\because y_{n}=\dfrac{n \lambda D}{d}\right]}$
${\Rightarrow n=2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: If the monochromatic source in Young's double slit experiment is replaced by white light, then
explanation:
At central point on screen, path difference is zero for all wavelengths. So, central bright fringe is white and other fringes depend on wavelength as ${\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}}$.
Therefore, other fringes will be coloured. Correct option is (3).

options.text: There will be a central dark fringe surrounded by a few coloured fringes
There will be a central bright white fringe surrounded by a few coloured fringes
All bright fringes will be of equal width

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE with Light of Two or More Wavelengths

question: In Young's double slit experiment, an electron beam is used to produce interference fringes of width ${\beta_{1}}$. Now the electron beam is replaced by a beam of protons with the same experimental set-up and same speed. The fringe width obtained is ${\beta_{2}}$. The correct relation between ${\beta _1}$ and ${\beta_{2}}$ is
explanation:
Fringe width is given by ${\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}}$
$\Rightarrow \beta \propto \lambda$
de-Broglie wavelength is given by
${\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{m}}$
${\Rightarrow m_{e} < m_{p} \Rightarrow \lambda_{e}>\lambda_{p} \quad \therefore \beta_{1}>\beta_{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: The ratio of intensities at two points $P$ and $Q$ on the screen in a Young's double slit experiment where phase difference between two waves of same amplitude are $\pi / 3$ and $\pi / 2$, respectively are
explanation:
Resultant intensity, $I_{\text {net }}=I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi$
where $\phi=$ phase difference.
$I_{1}=I_{0}+I_{0}+2 I_{0} \cos \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ for $\left(\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$
$I_{1}=2 I_{0}+2 I_{0} \times \dfrac{1}{2}=3 I_{0}$$I_{2}=I_{0}+I_{0}+2 I_{0} \cos \dfrac{\pi}{2}=2 I_{0}$ for $\left(\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Hence, ratio between the two waves
$=\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{I_{0}}{I_{0}}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: The width of fringe is $2\,mm$  on the screen in a double slits experiment for the light of wavelength of $400\,nm$. The width of the fringe for the light of wavelength $600\,nm$ will be
explanation:
Fringe width $w=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$.
For the same experimental set up, $w \alpha \lambda$ $\therefore \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{W_1}}} = \frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{\lambda }$
$ \Rightarrow {W_2} = \frac{{600}}{{400}} \times 2\,mm = 3\,mm$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a Young's double slit experiment, two slits are illuminated with a light of wavelength 800 nm . The line joining $A_{1} P$ is perpendicular to $A_{1} A_{2}$ as shown in the figure. If the first minimum is detected at $P$, the value of slits separation '$a$' will be the distance of screen from slits $D = 5\,cm.$
explanation:
Wavelength of light $(\lambda ) = 800\,nm$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955565/preptracker/questions/q_32532_explanation_1764955564701.png Distance of screen from slit
$(D) = 5\,cm = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
Distance between the slits $=(a)$
$\because$ We have got $I^{\text {st }}$ minimum at point $P$,
So, optical path difference,
${A_2}P - {A_1}P = \frac{\lambda }{2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\because A_{2} P=\sqrt{D^{2}+a^{2}}$ and $A_{1} P=D$
$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {{D^2} + {a^2}} - D = \frac{\lambda }{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{D^{2}+a^{2}}=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}+D$
$\Rightarrow\left(D^{2}+a^{2}\right)=\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{2}+D\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow D^{2}+a^{2}=\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4}+D^{2}+\dfrac{2 \lambda}{2} \times D$
$ \Rightarrow {a^2} = \frac{{{\lambda ^2}}}{4} + D\lambda \quad \left\{ {\because \frac{{{\lambda ^2}}}{4} < < D\lambda } \right\}$
So, $a^{2}=D \lambda \Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 800 \times 10^{-9}$
${a^2} = 4000 \times {10^{ - 11}}\,{m^2}$
$a = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,m$
or $a = 0.2\,mm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the position of $5^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe from the central maximum is $5 m$ . The distance between slit and screen is $1 m$ and wavelength of used monochromatic light is $600 nm$ . The separation between the slits is
explanation:
Distance of $n^{\text {th }}$ fringe from central fringe $y = \frac{{nD\lambda }}{d}\,\,\,[{\rm{where }}n = 1,2, \ldots .]$
Here, $y = 5\,cm,n = 5,D = 1\,m,\lambda = 600\,nm$
Let $d$ is separation between the slits.
$5 \times {10^{ - 2}} = \frac{{5 \times 1 \times 600 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{d}$
$ \Rightarrow d = 60\mu m$ (using (1))
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question: In a Young's double slits experiment, the ratio of amplitude of light coming from slits is $2: 1$. The ratio of the maximum to minimum intensity in the interference pattern is
explanation:
As for YDSE pattern,
$I_{\text {max }}=\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}, I_{\text {min }}=\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}\left[\because I \alpha a^{2}\right]$
$\dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\dfrac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{(2 x+x)^{2}}{(2 x-x)^{2}}=\dfrac{9 x^{2}}{x^{2}}$
$\dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\text {min }}}=\dfrac{9}{1}$ or $I_{\max }: I_{\min }$ is $9: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: One of the condition for interference is that the two sources should be very narrow.

Reason: One broad source is equal to large number of narrow sources.
explanation: A broad source is equivalent to a large number of narrow sources arranged side by side. Each of these sets of sources generates its own distinct interference pattern resulting in general illumination. This situation underscores the necessity of employing a narrow slit for interference experiments. So correct option is $(1)$.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: Two coherent source always have constant phase relationship.

Reason: Light from two coherent sources is reaching the screen. If the path difference at a point on the screen for yellow light is $3 \lambda / 2$ then the fringe at that point will be bright.
explanation:
Two coherent sources maintain a constant phase relationship, leading to stable phase relationships on the screen.
$\Delta x=\dfrac{3 \lambda}{2}$ corresponds to condition for 'darkfringe' is being fulfilled $\left[=(2 n+1) \dfrac{\lambda}{2}\right]$ where $n=1$. Reason is false
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: If the phase difference between the light waves emerging from the slits of the Young's experiment is $\pi$ radian. The central fringe will be dark.

Reason: Phase difference and path difference are not related.
explanation:
The central fringe in Young's experiment is dark when the phase difference $(\Delta \phi)$ between light waves from the slits is $\pi$ radians, as it corresponds to a path difference resulting in darkness $(\Delta x)$.
As $\lambda$ corresponds to $2 \pi$, where
$\Delta \phi=\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)(\Delta x)$
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: In interference all the fringes are of same width.

Reason: In interference fringe width is independent of position of the fringe.
explanation: The fringe width, represented by $\beta$ and calculated using the expression $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$, is unaffected by the specific position of the fringe. It gives consistent fringe width across the interference pattern. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: All bright interference bands have the same intensity.

Reason: In interference all bands receive same light from two sources.
explanation:
Condition for interference is 'coherence' not amplitude. All bright interference bands have consistent intensity in interference, as they receive the same amount of light from coherent sources. Although, generally both sources have nearly same amplitude, we have $\left(a_{1} \neq a_{2}\right)$
$I_{\text {bright }} \propto\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}$
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: In Young's experiment the fringe width is directly proportional to wavelength of the source used.

Reason: When a thin transparent sheet is placed in front of both the slits of Young's experiment, the fringe width will increase.
explanation: $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$ In Young's experiment, the fringe width $(\beta)$ is directly proportional to the source wavelength $(\lambda)$. Introducing a thin transparent sheet shifts whole fringe pattern by $\left[\dfrac{D}{d}(\mu-1) t\right]$ without altering the fringe width. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Two point coherent sources of light $S_{1}$, and $S_{2}$ are placed on a line as shown. $P$ and $Q$ are two points on that line. If at point $P$ maximum intensity is observed then maximum intensity should also be observed at $Q$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955554/preptracker/questions/q_32432_col1_1764955553672.png

Reason: In the figure of assertion if the distance $\left| {{S_1}P - {S_2}P} \right|$ is equal to distance $\left| {{S_2}Q - {S_1}Q} \right|$ then maximum intensity is observed at both $P$ and $Q$.
explanation: If maximum intensity is observed at point $P$, then in order for maximum intensity to also be observed at point $\mathrm{Q}$, The path difference shall be $\left|S_{1} Q-S_{2} Q\right|=\left|S_{1} P-S_{2} P\right|=n \lambda$. There is no generate that has maximum intensity always. So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringes become indistinct if one of the slits is covered with cellophane paper.

Reason: The cellophane paper decrease the wavelength of light.
explanation: When one of the slits is covered with cellophane paper, the intensity of light emerging from that slit is decreased because cellophane paper is translucent. So, the two interfering beams have different intensities or amplitudes. Therefore, the intensity at the minima will not be zero, leading to indistinct fringes. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: For best contrast between maxima and minima in the interference pattern of Young's double slit experiment, the intensity of light emerging out of the two slits should be equal.

Reason: The intensity of interference pattern is proportional to amplitude.
explanation:
fringe visibility $V=\left[\dfrac{I_{\max }-I_{\min }}{I_{\max }+I_{\min }}\right]$
$I \propto a^{2}, I_{\max } \propto\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}$
$I_{\text {min }} \propto\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}$
Where $a_{1}$ and $a_{2}$ are amplitudes.
For best visibility, $a_{1}=a_{2}$
So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Thin film which appears bright in reflected system will appear dark in the transmitted light and vice versa.

Reason: The conditions for film to appear bright or dark in reflected light are just reverse to those in the transmitted light.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955551/preptracker/questions/q_32404_explanation_1764955551237.png We know a phase difference of $\pi$ (or equivalently path difference of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$) occurs when light enters from a medium with higher refractive index. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955553/preptracker/questions/q_32404_explanation_1764955552822.png So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: In Young's double slit experiment using white light, the central fringe is white, the violet coloured fringe will be seen nearest to the central fringe.

Reason: The interference patterns due to different component colours of white light overlap throughout the screen.
explanation: If a monochromatic light source is replaced by a white light source in Young's interference experiment, then the central fringe appears white, and the fringe immediately adjacent to the central fringe is violet, since violet has least wavelength and hence its fringe will be smallest and closest to the centre. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Given, In Young's double slit experiment the two slits are at distance $d$ apart. Interference pattern is observed on a screen at distance $D$ from the slits. At a point on the screen when it is directly opposite to one of the slits, first dark fringe is observed.

Reason: The wavelength of wave is proportional to square of distance of two slits.
explanation:
When dark fringe is obtained at the point opposite to one of the slits then
$S_{1} P=D$
and $S_{2} P=\sqrt{D^{2}+d^{2}}$$=D\left(1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{D^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}=D\left(1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 D^{2}}\right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955550/preptracker/questions/q_32392_explanation_1764955549923.png Path difference $=S_{2} P-S_{1} P=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ for the first dark fringe
$D\left(1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 D^{2}}\right)-D=\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 D}=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{d^{2}}{D} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto d^{2}$
So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: YDSE when Line Joining the Sources is Parallel to the Screen

question:
Assertion: The pattern and position of fringes always remains same even after the introduction of transparent medium like glass plate in a path of one of the slit.

Reason: The central fringe is bright or dark is depends upon the initial phase difference between the two coherence source.
explanation: If a transparent medium of thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu$ is introduced into the path of one of the slits, the effective path in air is increased by an amount $(\mu-1)$ t due to the introduction of the plate. As a result, the whole fringe shifts by a distance $\left\{\dfrac{D}{d}(\mu-1) t\right\}$ towards the slit at which the glass plate is. In such cases, the fringe width remains unchanged, However whether the central fringe is bright or dark still depends on the initial phase difference between the two coherent sources. So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: Figure shows a double slit experiment, $P$ and $Q$ are the two coherent sources. The path lengths $P Y$ and $Q Y$ are $n \lambda$ and $(n+4) \lambda$ respectively where $n$ is whole number and $\lambda$ is wavelength. Taking the central bright fringe has zero path difference, what is formed at $Y$ ?
explanation: $\Delta x=n \lambda$.

question: Young's double slit experiment is conducted with light of wavelength $\lambda$. The intensity of the bright fringe is $I_{0}$. The intensity at a point where path difference is $\lambda / 4$ is given by
explanation:
$I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}, \theta=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} x$
$\therefore \theta=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \dfrac{\lambda}{4}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
$I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\pi}{4}=I_{0} \times \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{I_{0}}{2}$.

question: The young's double slit experiment is performed with light of wavelength ${6000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } }$ where in 16 fringes occupy a certain region on the screen. If 24 fringes occupy the same region with another light of wavelength $\lambda$, then $\lambda$ is:
explanation:
$n_{1} \lambda_{1}=n_{2} \lambda_{2}$ Position of fringe $=\dfrac{n D \lambda}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\dfrac{6000}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{24}{16}$
${\lambda _2} = \frac{{6000 \times 16}}{{{n_2}}} = \frac{{96000}}{{24}} = 4 \times {10^3} = 4000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } {\rm{ }}$.

question: In young's double slit experiment, the phase difference between the light waves reaching a point where third bright fringe forms will be $\left( {\lambda  = 6000\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } } \right)$
explanation: The phase difference between the light waves reaching the third bright fringe from the central bright fringe will be $\Delta \phi=2 n \pi ; n=3$. $\Delta \phi=2 \times 3 \times \pi=6 \pi$ (case of constructive interference)

question: The width of two slits in young's experiment are in the ratio of $9: 4$. What will be the intensity ratio of maxima and minima in the interference pattern?
explanation:
Intensity ratio $=\dfrac{(a+b)^{2}}{(a-b)^{2}}$
$\therefore$ Intensity ratio $=\left(\dfrac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{3}{2}+1}{\dfrac{3}{2}-1}\right)^{2}$
Intensity ratio $=25: 1$.

question: Two slits are seperated by $0.3\,mm$. A beam of $500\,mm$ light strikes the slits producing an interference pattern. The number of maxima observed in the angular range $-30^{\circ} < \theta < 30^{\circ}$.
explanation:
$0.3 \times 10^{-3} \times \sin 30^{\circ}=n \times 500 \times 10^{-9}$
$\Rightarrow n=300$
$\therefore 299+299+1=599$.

question: Two identical narrow slits $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda$ from a point source $P$. An interference pattern is produced on the screen as shown in figure. The condition for destructive interference at $Q$ is that ( $n$ is an integer)
explanation:
$\Delta x=(2 n+1) \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
$\Delta x=\left(l_{1}+l_{3}\right)-\left(l_{2}+l_{4}\right)$
$=(2 n+1) \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

question: In Young's double slit experiment, the wavelength of red light is $7800\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } $ and that of the blue light is  $5200\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } $. The value of $n$ for which $n^{\text {th }}$ bright band due to red light coincides with $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ bright band due to blue light, is
explanation:

$\Rightarrow n .7800=(n+1) 5200$
$\Rightarrow n=2$.

question: In $Y D S E$ how many maxima can be obtained on the screen if wavelength of light used is $200\,nm$ and $d = 700\,nm$.
explanation:

$\sin \theta=\dfrac{n \times 200}{700}=\dfrac{2}{7} n$
so, $n=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3$ are possible.

question: In Young's double slit experiment 62 fringes are visible in the field of view with sodium light $\left( {\lambda  = 5893{\rm{ }}\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } } \right)$. $\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \delta=7 \beta-\dfrac{9 \beta}{2}=\dfrac{5 \beta}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\beta}{\lambda}(\mu 1) t=\dfrac{5}{2} \beta \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{(2.5) \lambda}{\mu-1}\end{aligned}$

question: In Young's experiment, two coherent sources are placed $0.90\,mm$ apart and fringes are observed one metre away. If it produces second dark fringe at a distance of $1\,mm$ from central fringe, the wavelength of monochromatic light is used would be:
explanation:
For dark fringe $x=(2 n-1) \dfrac{\lambda D}{2 d}$
$\therefore \lambda = \frac{{2xd}}{{(2n - 1)D}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 0.9 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{(2 \times 2 - 1) \times 1}}$
$\lambda = 0.6 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,m = 6 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,cm.$

question: The path difference between two interfering light waves meeting at a point on the screen is $\left(\dfrac{87}{2}\right) \lambda$. The band obtained at that point is
explanation:
Path difference $=87\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{2}\right)$
As it is odd multiple of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$, that band is dark.
For dark point, $(2 n-1) \dfrac{\lambda}{2}=87\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{2}\right)$
$2 n-1=87 \Rightarrow 2 n=88 \Rightarrow n=44$
Hence, $44^{\text {th }}$ dark band is formed there.
subject: Physics

question: In young's double slit experiment, the two slits are '$d$' distance apart. Interference pattern is observed on the screen at a distance '$D$' from the slits. First dark fringe is observed on the screen directly opposite to one of the slits. The wavelength of the light is
explanation:
For $n^{\text {th }}$ dark fringe, $(2 n-1) \dfrac{\lambda D}{2 d}=\dfrac{d}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{d^{2}}{(2 n-1) D}$
For first dark fringe, $n=1$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{d^{2}}{D}$

question:
The optical path difference between two identical light waves arriving at a point is $31.5\,\lambda $ where ' $\lambda$ ' is the wavelength of light used. The point is
[Two light sources are coherent]
explanation:
Given $\Delta x=31.5 \lambda$
so, $\Delta x=63\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ The above is an odd multiple of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
$\Rightarrow$ A minimum will be formed and hence the point will be dark.
